Guide

# normalize-path (opens new window) (opens new window) (opens new window) (opens new window)

Normalize slashes in a file path to be posix/unix-like forward slashes. Also condenses repeat slashes to a single slash and removes and trailing slashes, unless disabled.

Please consider following this project's author, Jon Schlinkert (opens new window), and consider starring the project to show your ❤️ and support.

# Install

Install with npm (opens new window):

$ npm install --save normalize-path

# Usage

const normalize = require('normalize-path');

console.log(normalize('\\foo\\bar\\baz\\')); 
//=> '/foo/bar/baz'

win32 namespaces

console.log(normalize('\\\\?\\UNC\\Server01\\user\\docs\\Letter.txt')); 
//=> '//?/UNC/Server01/user/docs/Letter.txt'

console.log(normalize('\\\\.\\CdRomX')); 
//=> '//./CdRomX'

Consecutive slashes

Condenses multiple consecutive forward slashes (except for leading slashes in win32 namespaces) to a single slash.

console.log(normalize('.//foo//bar///////baz/')); 
//=> './foo/bar/baz'

# Trailing slashes

By default trailing slashes are removed. Pass false as the last argument to disable this behavior and keep trailing slashes:

console.log(normalize('foo\\bar\\baz\\', false)); //=> 'foo/bar/baz/'
console.log(normalize('./foo/bar/baz/', false)); //=> './foo/bar/baz/'

# Release history

# v3.0

No breaking changes in this release.

  • a check was added to ensure that win32 namespaces (opens new window) are handled properly by win32 path.parse() after a path has been normalized by this library.
  • a minor optimization was made to simplify how the trailing separator was handled

# About

Contributing

Pull requests and stars are always welcome. For bugs and feature requests, please create an issue.

Running Tests

Running and reviewing unit tests is a great way to get familiarized with a library and its API. You can install dependencies and run tests with the following command:

$ npm install && npm test
Building docs

(This project's readme.md is generated by verb (opens new window), please don't edit the readme directly. Any changes to the readme must be made in the .verb.md readme template.)

To generate the readme, run the following command:

$ npm install -g verbose/verb#dev verb-generate-readme && verb

# Related projects

Other useful path-related libraries:

  • contains-path (opens new window): Return true if a file path contains the given path. | homepage (opens new window)
  • is-absolute (opens new window): Returns true if a file path is absolute. Does not rely on the path module… more (opens new window) | homepage (opens new window)
  • is-relative (opens new window): Returns true if the path appears to be relative. | homepage (opens new window)
  • parse-filepath (opens new window): Pollyfill for node.js path.parse, parses a filepath into an object. | homepage (opens new window)
  • path-ends-with (opens new window): Return true if a file path ends with the given string/suffix. | homepage (opens new window)
  • unixify (opens new window): Convert Windows file paths to unix paths. | homepage (opens new window)
